definitions: (gerontology) The accumulation of damage to macromolecules, cells, tissues and organs with the passage of time; the progressing loss of health, mobility, vibrancy and body functionality, resulting in biological death.

The process of becoming older or more mature.
Wear; wear and tear.
present participle and gerund of age
Becoming elderly.
examples: The aging artist could no longer steadily hold the brush.

definitions: (economics, finance) The premium or percentage on a better sort of money when it is given in exchange for an inferior sort. The premium or discount on foreign bills of exchange is sometimes called agio.
examples: Owing to the enormous number of half-shekel coins required each year in Adar, these were greatly in demand, and the money-changers made a small fixed charge of an agio for changing whole into half shekels.

definitions: To cause to move with a violent, irregular action; to shake. [from 16th c.]
To disturb or excite; to perturb or stir up (a person). [from 16th c.]

definitions: simple past and past participle of agitate
(chemistry) Of a solution or substance, violently and chaotically moving around, such as due to being shaken.

definitions: present participle and gerund of agitate

definitions: Any of the genus Aglaonema of tropical foliage plants.

definitions: Glittering.

definitions: (sometimes figurative) glowing; radiant
examples: The fall wind stirs and the earth is aglow. Everything is golden.

definitions: (zoology) Belonging or pertaining to the superclass Agnatha, the jawless vertebrates.
examples: Haikouichthys is one of three species of jawless (or agnathan) fish to be found in the Early Cambrian period.

definitions: of the male bloodline; patrilineal
examples: agnatic primogeniture

definitions: (grammar) The property of being agnate.
consanguinity by a line of males only, as distinguished from cognation

definitions: Non- Oxford British English standard spelling of agnize.

definitions: An additional cognomen given, as an honour, to a Roman citizen.
(especially theology) Ignorance.

A person who holds to a form of agnosticism, especially uncertainty of the existence of a deity.
examples: The Agnostic is one who asserts—what no one denies—that there are limits to the sphere of human intelligence.

definitions: Of or relating to agnosticism or its adherents.

definitions: The view that absolute truth or ultimate certainty is unattainable, especially regarding knowledge not based on experience or perceivable phenomena.

definitions: (chiefly of eyes) Wide open.

definitions: An admixture; something added which stains, taints etc.

A metal that is a combination of two or more elements, at least one of which is a metal, a base metal.
examples: The sole grievance and alloy thus removed in the prospect of Harriet’s welfare, she was really in danger of becoming too happy for security.
Metrodorus said that in sadnesse there is some aloy of pleasure.
gold without alloy

SETH KITANGE TELEVISION AND RADIO Upheaval at CBS. […] Bill Moyers, a CBS News commentator and special correspondent, expressed his dismay in an interview with Newsweek in which he said, “Television news has never been pure. It has always been an alloy of journalism and show business.”

definitions: (figurative) To impair or debase by mixture.
To mix or combine; often used of metals.
To reduce the purity of by mixing with a less valuable substance.
examples: to alloy pleasure with misfortunes
to alloy gold with silver or copper, or silver with copper

definitions: simple past and past participle of alloy
mixed.
examples: Their pleasure was alloyed with misfortune.

definitions: Pimenta dioica, an evergreen tree of tropical America with aromatic berries.
A spice; the dried and ground unripe fruit of Pimenta dioica, thought to combine the flavours of several spices, such as cinnamon, nutmeg and cloves.
To refer to something indirectly or by suggestion; to invoke it by implication rather than mention.
examples: These speeches... do seem to allude unto such ministerial garments as were then in use.
It was aptly said by Newton that "whatever is not deduced from facts must be regarded as hypothesis," but hypothesis appears to us a title too honourable for the crude guessings to which we allude.

We live our lives in three dimensions for our threescore and ten allotted years. Yet every branch of contemporary science, from statistics to cosmology, alludes to processes that operate on scales outside of human experience: the millisecond and the nanometer, the eon and the light-year.

definitions: The walkway along the top of a castle wall, sometimes entirely covered and normally behind a parapet; the wall walk.
The power to attract, entice; the quality causing attraction.
examples: The swing, the gait, the pose, the allure of these men.

definitions: To entice; to attract.
examples: A tender voice his wondring ear allur'd.
Injustice doth allure them; as the honour of their vertuous actions enticeth the good.
[They retained] their ſweet skill in wonted melody; / Which euer after they abuſd to ill, / T’ allure weake trueillers, whom gotten they did kill.

definitions: An enticement, inducement or bait. [from 16th c.]
Attractiveness; appeal, charisma. [from 16th c.]
examples: He read the letter aloud. Sophia listened with the studied air of one for whom, even in these days, a title possessed some surreptitious allurement.
That though their Thoughts are ever turn'd upon appearing Aimiable, yet every Feature of their Faces and every Part of their Dress is fill'd with Snares and Allurements.
The sheer unimaginableness of coming into her mouth — of coming into anything other than the air or a tissue or a dirty sock — was an allurement too stupendous for a novice to forswear.

definitions: The act or habit of enticing or attracting in order to seek a benefit or advantage.
examples: Lookout heights and Smoky Mountains have allurings all their own.
Was this poor breast, from Love's allurings free, / Cruel to all, and gentle unto thee?

For, as when the red-cheeked, dancing girls, April and May, trip home to the wintry, misanthropic woods; even the barest, ruggedest, most thunder-cloven old oak will at least send forth some few green sprouts, to welcome such glad-hearted visitants; so Ahab did, in the end, a little respond to the playful allurings of that girlish air.

definitions: present participle and gerund of allure
Having the power to allure.
examples: Captain Edward Carlisle, soldier as he was, martinet as he was, felt a curious sensation of helplessness seize upon him as he met her steady gaze, her alluring smile; he could not tell what this prisoner might do.

definitions: An indirect reference; a hint; a reference to something supposed to be known, but not explicitly mentioned.
examples: In order to understand some of the allusions readers must remember that the Autobiography was originally written in the form of "epistles addressed to Mr. Robert Riddell at London, by his friend and relative," to which, in their collected form, the author had given the title "Obscurities in Private Life Developed; or, Robinson Crusoe Untravelled."
The influence of Lucretius on Virgil was pervasive, especially in Virgil's Georgics; and it is in clear allusion to Lucretius that Virgil wrote, “Happy is the man who can read the causes of things.”

I remember, before the Dwarf left the Queen, he followed us one day into thoſe gardens, and my Nurſe having ſet me down, he and I being cloſe together, near ſome Dwarf Apple trees, I muſt need ſhew my Wit, by a ſilly Alluſion between him and the Trees, which happens to hold in their Language as it doth in ours.

definitions: that contains or makes use of allusions (indirect references or hints)
examples: The Book is a more allusive work than the Tale, which leads to speculation on whether the digressions in both works might not merely be a case of a rambling narrator.
The footnotes ensure that the lines become more allusive and more polysemantic, vacillating between transubstantiation and ghostly intimations.

Around they go, taking in the poem's allusive system of images and its narrative density, the emotional heat of its subject matter, its increasing cultural salience re: women, re: trauma, re: bodies, re: life at the end of the world.

English poetry is compelled by the stubbornness of the language continually to renounce the too obviously poetic: but in seeking to be more precise, more dense and more allusive, Russian poetry has never had to give up the straightforward traditional intoxications of sound and rhyme.

definitions: The quality of being allusive.

definitions: soil, clay, silt or gravel deposited by flowing water, as it slows, in a river bed, delta, estuary or flood plain

definitions: Chiefly followed by with: to enter into an alliance or unite for a common aim.

[A]ftervvards he [Constantine the Great] allied vvith Licinius, and gave him his Siſter in marriage, and acknovvledged him his Colleague in the Empire.

And novv the VVarriors paſſing on the vvay, / The graceful Paris firſt excus'd his Stay. / To vvhom the noble Hector thus reply'd: / O Chief! in Blood, and novv in Arms, ally'd! / Thy povv'r in VVar vvith Juſtice none conteſt; / Knovvn is thy Courage, and thy Strength confeſt.

[W]hen hee's remou'd, your Highneſſe / VVill take againe your Queene, as yours at firſt, / Euen for your Sonnes ſake, and thereby for ſealing / The Iniurie of Tongues, in Courts and Kingdomes / Knovvne, and ally'd to yours.

definitions: Baldness.

At least 40% of women experience hair loss or alopecia over their lifetimes. This could be alopecia areata (patchy hair loss), traction alopecia (strained hair loss) or another form. The different ways that women’s hair loss has been depicted across art history demonstrates the many different ways it has been interpreted over the years. In 16th and 17th century Britain, for example, women’s alopecia was sometimes interpreted as retribution for sins, including adultery.

definitions: Relating to or suffering from alopecia.

definitions: (intransitive, poetic) To depart; to go to another place.

definitions: (baseball, following the noun modified) Out.

definitions: A feeling of fear and reverence.
A feeling of amazement.
examples: Last spring, the periodical cicadas emerged across eastern North America. Their vast numbers and short above-ground life spans inspired awe and irritation in humans—and made for good meals for birds and small mammals.

In 1825, the first public railway carried passengers across the English countryside, setting in motion not just an engineering revolution, but an industrial one too. Imagine the awe and excitement of those first passengers as they boarded the train, unaware that they were witnessing the dawn of a new era.

For several minutes no one spoke; I think they must each have been as overcome by awe as was I. All about us was a flora and fauna as strange and wonderful to us as might have been those upon a distant planet had we suddenly been miraculously transported through ether to an unknown world.

definitions: To control by inspiring dread.
To inspire fear and reverence in.
examples: That large room had always awed Ivor: even as a child he had never wanted to play in it, for all that it was so limitless, the parquet floor so vast and shiny and unencumbered, the windows so wide and light with the fairy expanse of Kensington Gardens.

What Miss Anna Seward called 'the wit and aweless impoliteness of the stupendous creature' [Samuel Johnson] bore down every one before it. ‘I forget your coat of arms.’ ‘A human foot d’or, in a field azure; the foot crushes a serpent rampant whose fangs are imbedded in the heel.’

definitions: (mineralogy) A blue vitreous mineral; a basic hydrated copper carbonate, with the chemical formula Cu 3 (C O 3) 2 (O H) 2, used since antiquity as a blue pigment or ores to extract metallic copper.
(anatomy, medicine) In anatomy, a structure that is unpaired. This is relatively unusual, as most elements of anatomy reflect bilateral symmetry.
(biology) In genetics, not containing a specific allele of a gene, or only having wild type alleles (cf. heterozygous, homozygous).
(biology) Odd; not one of a pair; single.
examples: the azygous muscle of the uvula
